item.title: next_id scans sprints.jsonl with per-line jq — slow on large archives
item.intent: ID generation must remain fast as the sprint archive grows — per-line jq invocations in a shell loop are O(n) and will become noticeable at hundreds of entries.
item.description: In next_id(), the archive max-ID computation uses 'jq -r ... archive_file | sort -rn | head -1'. This invokes jq once on the whole JSONL file, which is correct. However the BACKLOG_FILES loop calls jq once per file, and the archive jq reads all lines just to get the max numeric suffix. For large archives (hundreds of sprint entries), this is still fast, but the JSONL slurp approach (jq -r '[...] | max') is more robust than relying on sort -rn with potentially non-numeric values. The real risk is that if a non-numeric suffix appears in itemId (e.g. 'FEAT-new'), jq tonumber throws an error that is silently swallowed by 2>/dev/null, hiding ID conflicts. Fix: add error handling and filter to numeric-only suffixes before max.
steps: In the archive scan, change the jq pipeline to filter to numeric IDs only: jq -r --arg pfx "$prefix-" '[.itemId | select(startswith($pfx)) | ltrimstr($pfx) | select(test("^[0-9]+$")) | tonumber] | max // 0'
Apply the same numeric-only filter in the BACKLOG_FILES loop: add | select(test("^[0-9]+$")) before tonumber
Verify: next_id FEAT still returns FEAT-023 (or correct next ID) with numeric items; non-numeric IDs in archive are silently skipped
acceptanceCriteria: next_id correctly skips non-numeric ID suffixes without crashing
next_id still returns the correct next sequential ID for FEAT and BUG prefixes
No regression in ID generation for existing items
